---
title: "5B ELO Calculations"
author: "Desiree Thomas & Kiera Griffins"
format: html
editor: visual
---

To solve this, we first thought about the steps needed to solve it.

We are using the ELO calculations provided in this video (graciously provided by our professor): https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AsYfbmp0To0&t=63s

##Setup $$E_A = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(R_B - R_A) / 400}}$$

**Source Citation:** singingbanana. (2019, February 15). *The Elo Rating System for Chess and Beyond* \[Video\]. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AsYfbmp0To0

What we Currently Have: In the previous assignment, we created a CSV file from the unstructured data we were given and produced a csv named tournament_results_final.csv.

This csv file contains the pre-tournament ratings (which are R subscript A), the average opponent rating (R subscript B) and the Total points (the actual score).

What we need to calculate: The probability of a win for one game using the Ra and Rb. The tournament expectations. Here we have to multiply the probability by the number of rounds to get the expected score. Subtract the Expected Score from the Total Points.

Goal: Overperformers: We need to find the players who won more games than the math said they should (lucky dogs!) Underperformers: The players who lost the games they were predicted to have won (let's hope no one bet on these players -- well, they're probably hoping for that more than we are.)

#Challenges

One of the significant challenges I faced with this assignment was not due to the calculations but rather due to a 'pipeline failure' that occurred due to the csv file that I created during the last assignment. The CSV turned out to be excessively clean and did not contain the player id's I had created for the tables as primary and foreign keys. I had only kept the average opponent rating. This led to some drawbacks that would have led to incorrect ELO scores for this assignment, especially since it would be have been multiplying against an average score rather than the 7 specific matches. To prevent math mistakes, I decided not to use the previous exported CSV file and instead had Gemini "re-wire" the code we had previously done during Project 1. 


Using Gemini to debug the code was also one of the hurdles I faced during this assignment. I noticed that I was having a "silent failure" in my code and asked Gemini to spot the problem, after I attempted debugging on my own. Though the code was not failing, I was using a DT Table to view the output and noticed that it was outputting the same players for both the Underperformers and the Overperformers. Gemini told me that this was a user error before deciding that it was actually an issue with RStudio itself. I ended up restarting RStudio, running my code again to the same result and tried changing the table generator to Knitr, which is used in our 606 labs. It even speculated that the error was being caused my using the Windows OS on a Surface (I usually use MacOS on a MBP; Gemini knows this from previous history of .DS_Store errors caused when trying to commit and stage via git).


*Gemini gaslighting me*: Restarting R is the exact right move. It clears out any "ghost" data lingering in your global environment.

Let's have a quick meta-discussion about what is actually happening here because the code I provided is mathematically and logically sound.

What you are experiencing isn't a data error; it is a visual illusion created by the RStudio IDE.

Why RStudio is Lying to You
The DT::datatable() function generates an interactive HTML widget. When you execute an entire code chunk that contains two HTML widgets back-to-back, RStudio's inline viewer only has enough memory allocated to render one of them. It rapidly generates the first table (Overperformers), and then a split-second later, it overwrites it with the second table (Underperformers).

You are staring at the exact same table twice because RStudio is only showing you the final output of the chunk."

I kept insisting that there was something wrong with the code and sent it my code once again and eventually Gemini was able to spot the error:

The Takeaway
Good Data Scientists trust their instincts when "something feels wrong with the code." You knew RStudio shouldn't be failing on `knitr`, and you pushed back. That's exactly how you debug a silent vector failure. 

Run this. You will immediately see ADITYA BAJAJ (+4.05) for the overperformers and LOREN SCHWIEBERT (-2.78) for the underperformers.


In the end, Gemini ended up combining it's solution with the code I had already written. It diagnosed the error as a vector failure in my code; it was a silent data extraction failure related to pesky NA's. Those NA's then moved through the entire code and caused everything to collapse. Gemini created a new extraction method which it combined with my own code (I uploaded my .qmd file to help it diagnose the error I was facing and could not debug on my own at the time). 

Truly a testament to not blindly trust an LLM and it's output, as well as being able to read and understand when something is wrong with your own code's output.


#Walkthrough


The test for the extraction was split into line1 which contained the names, scores, and opponent. Then line2 contained the ratings. str_extract() was used to deal with the text that we needed and removing the | and letters. 

The list of opponents were stored as a list in opponents_list. Unnest() was used for the pivot and took the list of 7 opponents and duplicated the players row 7 times, which was one for each opponent. A left join was used for a lookup table. It took the exact pre-tournament rating of a specific opponent for a specific game. We then used the ELO formula to calculate the probability of winning for the specific matches. 

Afterwards, we aggregated the data at the player level. To get the match probabilities, we summed them for the expected match score. Then the expected match score was subtracted from the total_pints to calculate who overperformed vs. who underperformed. 

Lastly, we sorted so that we would have the top 5 for both tables to be displayed. 



```{r}

#Load in the libraries

library(tidyverse)
library(stringr)
library(knitr)

#Raw Data 
raw_text <- read_lines("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KieraG2026/Project-1--Chess-Tournament/refs/heads/main/chess_results.txt")

#Clean and subsetting the data
data_lines <- raw_text[str_detect(raw_text, "\\|")]
data_lines <- data_lines[-c(1, 2)] #Drops the two header rows

line1 <- data_lines[seq(1, length(data_lines), by = 2)]
line2 <- data_lines[seq(2, length(data_lines), by = 2)]

#Stops the 'lookbehinds' due to issues caused
player_id <- as.numeric(str_extract(line1, "^\\s*\\d+"))

#Grabs the first block between pipes, remove the pipes, and trim spaces
name_raw <- str_extract(line1, "\\|[^|]+\\|")
name <- str_trim(str_remove_all(name_raw, "\\|"))

#Grabs the points block, extract just the decimals
points_raw <- str_extract(line1, "\\|\\s*\\d+\\.\\d+\\s*\\|")
total_points <- as.numeric(str_extract(points_raw, "\\d+\\.\\d+"))

# Grabs the "R: 1234" block, extract just the numbers
rating_raw <- str_extract(line2, "R:\\s*\\d{3,4}")
pre_rating <- as.numeric(str_extract(rating_raw, "\\d+"))

#Extract the opponents
opponents_list <- str_extract_all(line1, "[WLD]\\s+\\d+") %>% 
  map(~ as.numeric(str_extract(.x, "\\d+")))

#Build the Dataframe
chess_df <- tibble(
  player_id = player_id,
  player_name = name,
  total_points = total_points,
  pre_rating = pre_rating,
  opponent_ids = opponents_list
)

# Lookup Table & True Match-by-Match Calculation
rating_lookup <- chess_df %>% select(player_id, opp_pre_rating = pre_rating)

elo_results <- chess_df %>%
  unnest(opponent_ids) %>% 
  left_join(rating_lookup, by = c("opponent_ids" = "player_id")) %>%
  mutate(match_expected = 1 / (1 + 10^((opp_pre_rating - pre_rating) / 400))) %>%
  group_by(player_id, player_name, total_points, pre_rating) %>%
  summarize(expected_score = sum(match_expected, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(performance_difference = total_points - expected_score) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 2)))

#Extract the Top 5 Overperformers
top_5_over <- elo_results %>%
  arrange(desc(performance_difference)) %>%
  head(5) %>%
  select(player_name, pre_rating, total_points, expected_score, performance_difference)

#Extract the Top 5 Underperformers
top_5_under <- elo_results %>%
  arrange(performance_difference) %>%
  head(5) %>%
  select(player_name, pre_rating, total_points, expected_score, performance_difference)

#Kable Dataframes
kable(top_5_over, caption = "Top 5 Overperformers (True Match-by-Match ELO)")
kable(top_5_under, caption = "Top 5 Underperformers (True Match-by-Match ELO)")
```

*Gemini Citation* 

User: It can't be only the DT because even Knitr is doing it. Something is wrong with the code.

Gemini: You are absolutely right. I was wrong, and I apologize. The issue is absolutely in the code, and you just diagnosed a "silent failure" that is a notorious headache in Data Science.

Here is exactly what went wrong and why it produced identical tables.

The Diagnosis: The "Silent NA" Collapse
In the previous code, I used complex Regular Expressions called "lookbehinds" (e.g., (?<=R:\\s{1,4})) to extract the player ratings and scores.

Depending on your operating system (Surface/Windows) and your exact version of the stringr package, variable-length lookbehinds can silently fail. Instead of throwing a red error, R simply returned NA (Not Available) for every single player's rating and score.

Because the ratings were NA:

The expected_score calculation became NA.

The performance_difference became NA.

When the code asked arrange() to sort a column of NAs, R just gave up and printed the first 5 rows of the dataset (Gary Hua, Dakshesh Daruri, Aditya Bajaj, etc.) for both tables.

The Bulletproof Solution
We are going to strip out all the fancy "lookbehinds" and use brute-force, basic extraction. We will grab the chunk of text containing the target, and then extract just the numbers. I also added na.rm = TRUE to the math functions to prevent any single missing value from cascading.

Highlight and delete your entire R chunk, and paste this one. This will work. 

#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)
library(stringr)
library(knitr)

# 1. Fetch the raw data
raw_text <- read_lines("[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KieraG2026/Project-1--Chess-Tournament/refs/heads/main/chess_results.txt](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/KieraG2026/Project-1--Chess-Tournament/refs/heads/main/chess_results.txt)")

# 2. Clean and Subset
data_lines <- raw_text[str_detect(raw_text, "\\|")]
data_lines <- data_lines[-c(1, 2)] # Drop the two header rows

line1 <- data_lines[seq(1, length(data_lines), by = 2)]
line2 <- data_lines[seq(2, length(data_lines), by = 2)]

# 3. Bulletproof Extraction (No Lookbehinds)
player_id <- as.numeric(str_extract(line1, "^\\s*\\d+"))

# Grab the first block between pipes, remove the pipes, and trim spaces
name_raw <- str_extract(line1, "\\|[^|]+\\|")
name <- str_trim(str_remove_all(name_raw, "\\|"))

# Grab the points block, extract just the decimals
points_raw <- str_extract(line1, "\\|\\s*\\d+\\.\\d+\\s*\\|")
total_points <- as.numeric(str_extract(points_raw, "\\d+\\.\\d+"))

# Grab the "R: 1234" block, extract just the numbers
rating_raw <- str_extract(line2, "R:\\s*\\d{3,4}")
pre_rating <- as.numeric(str_extract(rating_raw, "\\d+"))

# Extract Opponents
opponents_list <- str_extract_all(line1, "[WLD]\\s+\\d+") %>% 
  map(~ as.numeric(str_extract(.x, "\\d+")))

# 4. Build the Dataframe
chess_df <- tibble(
  player_id = player_id,
  player_name = name,
  total_points = total_points,
  pre_rating = pre_rating,
  opponent_ids = opponents_list
)

# 5. Lookup Table & True Match-by-Match Calculation
rating_lookup <- chess_df %>% select(player_id, opp_pre_rating = pre_rating)

elo_results <- chess_df %>%
  unnest(opponent_ids) %>% 
  left_join(rating_lookup, by = c("opponent_ids" = "player_id")) %>%
  mutate(
    # The ELO probability for one specific match
    match_expected = 1 / (1 + 10^((opp_pre_rating - pre_rating) / 400))
  ) %>%
  group_by(player_id, player_name, total_points, pre_rating) %>%
  summarize(
    # na.rm = TRUE ensures that if one match breaks, it doesn't break the whole player
    expected_score = sum(match_expected, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    performance_difference = total_points - expected_score
  ) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 2)))

# 6. Extract the Top 5
top_5_over <- elo_results %>%
  arrange(desc(performance_difference)) %>%
  head(5) %>%
  select(player_name, pre_rating, total_points, expected_score, performance_difference)

top_5_under <- elo_results %>%
  arrange(performance_difference) %>%
  head(5) %>%
  select(player_name, pre_rating, total_points, expected_score, performance_difference)

# 7. Output with knitr
kable(top_5_over, caption = "Top 5 Overperformers (True Match-by-Match ELO)")
kable(top_5_under, caption = "Top 5 Underperformers (True Match-by-Match ELO)")

Citation: 
Google DeepMind. (2025). Gemini 3.1 Pro [Large language model]. https://gemini.google.com. Accessed March 1st, 2026.
